context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 1

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 12th February, 2024
Days Covered: 01 and 02 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)

Please make note of the following instructions:
- Your solutions must be submitted to Gradescope as a single PDF file. While we allow handwritten solutions, we strongly suggest that you typeset in LaTeX, using the template available on Canvas.
- Each problem must be accompanied by a collaboration statement: the students you collaborated with, and any external resources you consulted when solving the problem. Do not leave this blank—instead write "collaborators: none" if appropriate. See the Course Information handout for more details on the collaboration policy.


Problem 1. Collaboration Policy [10 points]

Carefully read the collaboration policy in our Course Information Handout. Then, consider the scenarios described below, and discuss whether and why each one does or does not violate the course homework policies. If the issue is ambiguous, explain how you would resolve it. Simply answering "I'd check with the TA" for each answer is not sufficient.

(a) You host a pset party where ten of you work together to solve the problems before separating to write up your own solutions.
(b) You work with a friend on a problem, taking turns writing ideas and calculations jointly in the same notebook as you work out the solution, then photograph those notes so you can each have a copy of your sketch before you separate to write up your own solutions.
(c) You work together to figure out a detailed solution; one of you then dictates it to the other so that each of you can turn in a solution you've written yourself.
(d) After you've solved a problem, your friend who is still working on the problem asks you for feedback on a solution idea. Leveraging your knowledge of the right answer, you identify and point out a flaw in their proposed solution.
(e) After you've solved a problem, you try to help your friends by acting as a sounding board while they work towards the solution. With the deadline approaching, since they are still stuck, you allow them to skim your writeup to get a general idea of how to approach the problem, which allows them to go off and independently figure out their solution and write it up.
(f) After you've worked with your friend to solve a problem and each done your own writeup, you exchange writeups and examine them to identify and correct any flaws in one or the other.
(g) You remember seeing the homework problem in a problem set of a class you took previously. You look at your notes, see that you got a perfect grade, and copy your solution.
(h) You know the answer to the problem because you read it in a book you are reading to complement the required textbook, so you write it down that way.
(i) You know the answer to the problem because you read it in a book you are reading to complement the required textbook, so you explain the solution to your pset collaborators.
(j) While completing a late problem set for partial credit, you read the published solutions as you type your own answers, making sure to adjust the word choice and sentence structure as you go.


Problem 2. Set Formulas and Propositional Formulas [10 points]

(a) [5 pts] Use a truth table to verify that the propositional formula (P AND NOT Q) OR (P AND Q) is equivalent to P.
Note: For propositions, the three notations "NOT Q", "¬Q", and "Q-bar" are interchangeable.

(b) [5 pts] Prove that
    A = (A − B) ∪ (A ∩ B)
for all sets A, B, by showing
    x ∈ A   iff   x ∈ ((A − B) ∪ (A ∩ B))
for all elements x. How does this relate to part (a)?
Note: the set difference A − B is defined on page 105.


Problem 3. Predicate Practice [10 points]

For parts (a)–(d) below, translate the given statements into predicate logic, while preserving the meaning as closely as possible. In addition to boolean logic symbols like AND, OR, NOT, and IMPLIES, and quantifiers ∃ and ∀, you may build predicates using arithmetic, inequalities, and constants.

For this problem, you should use ℕ = {0, 1, 2, ...} as your domain of discourse: all variables are assumed to belong to ℕ, so ∃x and ∀x mean ∃x ∈ ℕ and ∀x ∈ ℕ. Avoid quantifying over other sets, such as "∀p ∈ Primes" or "∃n ≥ 1".

For example, "n is odd" could be translated as
    isOdd(n) := ∃m. (2m + 1 = n)
or, relatedly,
    isNotEven(n) := NOT (∃a. n = 2a).
The predicate "p is a prime number" could be translated to
    isPrime(p) := (p > 1) AND NOT (∃m ∃n. (m > 1 AND n > 1 AND mn = p)).
You may use the predicates isOdd(n) and isPrime(p) by name in your answers below.

(a) [2 pts] The predicate sixish(n) defined as "n is a natural number whose first digit is 6."
    Hint: You may wish to use exponents, e.g., 10^k. Be careful about where you do or don't need quantifiers.
(b) [2 pts] "There are exactly two natural numbers n such that Q(n) is true."
(c) [2 pts] Goldbach's Conjecture: "every even integer greater than 2 can be written as the sum of two primes."
    Note: Do not use quantifier shorthand like "∀n > 2. V(n)". Instead, use the more basic "∀n. W(n)" and include all necessary logic inside W(n).
    Hint: "If n is even and greater than 2, then ...".
(d) [2 pts] "Every prime larger than 100 is sixish."
    Hint: You may refer to "sixish" by name, even if you didn't solve part (a).
(e) [2 pts] Is the predicate R(n) := "∃b. n = 2b + 5" equivalent to isOdd(n)? Prove your answer.
    Hint: Recall that our domain of discourse is ℕ.


Problem 4. Induction and Contradiction [Edited] [20 points]

(a) [10 pts] Suppose Q(x) is a predicate defined for all x ∈ ℝ. Assume Q has the following property: for every x, y ∈ ℝ, if Q(x · y) is true, then at least one of Q(x) or Q(y) must be true.
From this, prove that Q satisfies the following stronger property: for any integer n ≥ 1 and any n numbers x1, x2, ..., xn ∈ ℝ, if Q(x1 · x2 · ... · xn) is true, then at least one of Q(x1), Q(x2), ..., Q(xn) must be true.
Prove the result by induction on n. Be sure to carefully identify your inductive hypothesis P(n), your base case(s), and the assumption and goal in your inductive step.
    Hint: x1 · x2 · ... · x_{n−1} · xn = (x1 · x2 · ... · x_{n−1}) · xn

(b) [5 pts] Consider the following theorem:
    Theorem 1. If the product of n ≥ 1 real numbers is irrational, then at least one of those numbers must be irrational.
We will eventually prove this Theorem by citing part (a). In preparation, what should we choose for Q from part (a)? What lemma must we prove about your chosen predicate Q before we're allowed to use part (a)?

(c) [5 pts] Use contradiction to carefully prove the lemma that you identified in part (b), and then finish the proof of Theorem 1.
While proving your lemma, you should not assume any prior knowledge about irrational numbers beyond the definition ("irrational" means "not rational"), but you may use familiar facts about rational numbers.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on this 6.1200 pset, and I’m pretty unsure about a few of the logic/proof questions. Could we start with Problem 1 or 2? I think 2(a) looks simpler, but I might need help not mixing up truth tables and set proofs.
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's start with 2(a). Before I say anything, give it a shot — what are the variables involved in the formula (P AND NOT Q) OR (P AND Q), and how would you set up the truth table?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to start with Problem 2(a), which is a straightforward truth table verification. Rather than explaining how to do it, I should ask them to attempt it first and see where they get stuck. This respects the Socratic method and guided discovery principles while keeping my response bite-sized.

exchanges.student:
The variables are just \(P\) and \(Q\), so I think there should be 4 rows total. I’d make columns for \(P\), \(Q\), then \(
eg Q\), then \(P \land 
eg Q\), \(P \land Q\), and finally \((P \land 
eg Q) \lor (P \land Q)\).
exchanges.tutor: That setup looks exactly right. Now go ahead and fill in the values — what do you get in each row?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the variables, the number of rows, and the column structure. This is a great setup. I should affirm this briefly and push them to actually fill in the table rather than just describing it.

exchanges.student:
Let me try:

- \(P=T, Q=T\): \(\neg Q=F\), \(P\land \neg Q=F\), \(P\land Q=T\), so final is \(T\)
- \(P=T, Q=F\): \(\neg Q=T\), \(P\land \neg Q=T\), \(P\land Q=F\), so final is \(T\)
- \(P=F, Q=T\): \(\neg Q=F\), both middle parts are \(F\), so final is \(F\)
- \(P=F, Q=F\): \(\neg Q=T\), and I think \(P\land \neg Q=T\)? Wait, no, since \(P\) is false that should still be \(F\), so final is \(F\)

So the last column is \(T, T, F, F\), which matches \(P\).
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right — and good catch on that last row. The final column matches P perfectly, so the equivalence is verified.

Now for 2(b), you need to prove A = (A−B) ∪ (A∩B) by showing x ∈ A iff x ∈ (A−B) ∪ (A∩B). How do you think the truth table you just built connects to this set proof?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly filled in the entire truth table and caught their own mistake in the last row, which shows good self-monitoring. They've completed 2(a). I should affirm this and naturally transition to 2(b), which builds on this result.

exchanges.student: I think it’s kind of the same pattern with membership as propositions? Like if \(P\) means “\(x\in A\)” and \(Q\) means “\(x\in B\),” then \(x\in A-B\) is like \(P\land \neg Q\), and \(x\in A\cap B\) is like \(P\land Q\). So the right-hand side becomes \((P\land \neg Q)\lor (P\land Q)\), which is equivalent to just \(P\), meaning \(x\in A\).
exchanges.tutor: That's the right insight. But an insight isn't yet a proof — you need to show x ∈ A iff x ∈ (A−B) ∪ (A∩B) formally. How would you structure that as a step-by-step argument using the definitions of ∪, ∩, and set difference?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made exactly the right conceptual connection between the truth table and the set proof. This is the core insight of 2(b). However, for a proper proof, they need to write this up as a formal biconditional argument (iff), not just state the analogy. I should push them to think about how to turn this insight into actual proof steps without writing it for them.

exchanges.student: I’m a little stuck on how formal they want it. I think I should start with \(x \in (A-B)\cup(A\cap B)\) and unpack definitions, but I’m not sure if I should do both directions separately or write one chain of iff statements.
exchanges.tutor: A chain of iff statements works well here — it handles both directions at once. Try starting with 'x ∈ (A−B) ∪ (A∩B)' and unpacking each definition one step at a time. What's the first iff step you'd write?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a reasonable structural question about proof format. I can point them toward the chain of iff statements approach since it's clean and natural here, without writing anything for them. This is a minor scaffolding nudge, not giving the answer.

exchanges.student:
Okay, first step I’d write:

\[
x \in (A-B)\cup(A\cap B) \iff (x\in A-B)\ \lor\ (x\in A\cap B).
\]

Then maybe

\[
\iff ((x\in A)\land (x\notin B)) \lor ((x\in A)\land (x\in B)).
\]

And then that should simplify to just \(x\in A\). I’m just not sure how explicit I need to be in that last simplification.
exchanges.tutor: You're almost there. What justifies that last simplification step? Think about what you already proved in part (a).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the chain almost complete. The last step is where they need to explicitly invoke the propositional equivalence from part (a) to justify the simplification. I should ask them to think about what justifies that last step, nudging them to connect back to part (a).

exchanges.student:
Oh, right — I can say let \(P\) be the statement \(x\in A\) and \(Q\) be \(x\in B\). Then

\[
((x\in A)\land (x\notin B)) \lor ((x\in A)\land (x\in B))
\]

has the same form as \((P\land \neg Q)\lor(P\land Q)\), which part (a) showed is equivalent to \(P\). So it’s equivalent to \(x\in A\). Is that enough, or do I need to restate the full biconditional cleanly at the end?
exchanges.tutor: The reasoning is solid. As for your question — what do you think the reader needs to see to confirm the original claim x ∈ A iff x ∈ (A−B) ∪ (A∩B) has been fully established?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has completed the logical chain correctly and is now asking a minor formatting question. This is good progress. I should affirm the substance and briefly nudge them to think about whether the reader needs a concluding line, without writing it for them.

exchanges.student:
I think I should end by explicitly writing the final biconditional, like

\[
x \in (A-B)\cup(A\cap B) \iff x\in A,
\]

and then since this holds for every \(x\), conclude \(A=(A-B)\cup(A\cap B)\). That part finally clicks. Could I do Problem 3(b) next?
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right — that closing line ties it all together nicely. Good work on Problem 2.

For 3(b), you need to express 'there are exactly two natural numbers n such that Q(n) is true.' Before writing anything, what does 'exactly two' mean logically — what conditions do you need to capture?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly completed Problem 2(b) and wants to move to 3(b). This is a reasonable transition. I should briefly affirm their conclusion on 2(b) and then prompt them to attempt 3(b) on their own first.

exchanges.student: I think it means there exist two numbers where \(Q\) is true, and also no other number has \(Q\) true. So maybe I need existence plus uniqueness-ish: there are \(a\) and \(b\), \(a \neq b\), \(Q(a)\), \(Q(b)\), and if \(Q(n)\) then \(n=a\) or \(n=b\).
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly the right breakdown. Now try writing it out as a single predicate logic formula using quantifiers and the symbols from the problem.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified all three components needed: existence of two distinct witnesses, Q holds for both, and any n satisfying Q must be one of them. This is the right logical structure. I should affirm this and push them to write it out formally in predicate logic.

exchanges.student:
I’d write:

\[
\exists a \exists b \big( a \neq b \land Q(a) \land Q(b) \land \forall n \,(Q(n) \rightarrow (n=a \lor n=b))\big).
\]

I think that says exactly two, unless I’m missing some annoying detail.
exchanges.tutor:
That's correct — no annoying details missing. The formula captures exactly two nicely.

Where would you like to go next? There are still several problems to work through.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's formula is correct and complete. All three components are properly captured. I should affirm this clearly and, since we've now done 2(a), 2(b), and 3(b), ask where they want to go next to keep momentum.

grade.overview: The tutor conducted a clean Socratic session across Problems 2(a), 2(b), and 3(b). At no point did the tutor give away answers; instead, it consistently prompted the student to produce each step. The student did the reasoning work throughout, and the tutor's confirmations were brief and accurate. No off-topic drift, no redundancy, no grading language, and responses were appropriately concise.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided a near-submission-ready answer, never rewrote student text, and never stated the key idea before the student. Every substantive step (truth table setup, iff chain, 'exactly two' decomposition) was generated by the student. 1.2: The tutor built on each correct student contribution and used targeted follow-up questions rather than method dumps. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., 'an insight isn't yet a proof — you need to show it formally') that directed the student's methodology. 2.1 and 2.2: No repetition without progress and no assignment misalignment occurred. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise, interactive, and supportive throughout, with no grading language.
